title: Fundamentals of Software Architecture
subtitle: An Engineering Approach
author: Mark Richards
description: A practical, vendor-neutral guide to software architecture as a set of trade-offs across structural patterns, runtime characteristics, four views of a system (4+1 model), eight core architecture characteristics, and nine fundamental architecture patterns (layered, microkernel, microservices, event-driven, space-based, pipeline, client-server, service-based, service-oriented), all grounded in the definition that architecture is the set of decisions hard to change. Includes a serious treatment of soft skills for architects, ADRs as documentation, and the sociotechnical reality of architecture.
keyIdeas: Software architecture is the set of decisions that are expensive to reverse — and the trade-offs behind them
Kruchten's 4+1 architectural view model (logical, process, physical, development + scenarios) as the best tool for describing systems
Eight core architecture characteristics grouped into operational, structural, and cross-cutting
Nine fundamental architecture patterns with uniform treatment: layered, microkernel, microservices, event-driven, space-based, pipeline, client-server, service-based, service-oriented
Component-based thinking precedes technology choice; logical components must be identified before physical patterns are assigned
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) as lightweight, durable documentation of architectural decisions
Conway's law: architecture mirrors the organization's communication structure
The 'it depends' answer is correct only when followed by an explicit, context-specific reason
keyTakeaways: Software architecture is fundamentally about trade-offs — there are no 'best' answers, only better or worse fits for a given context.
The 4+1 view model (Kruchten) gives a vocabulary for describing any system from four complementary angles plus the use cases that tie them together.

Architecture characteristics are non-functional requirements: availability, performance, scalability, reliability, recoverability, security, maintainability, and testability are the eight most commonly considered.
Trade-off thinking matters more than pattern matching: every pattern has strengths, weaknesses, and applicability conditions that must be weighed against the system's required characteristics.
Components are the unit of software composition. Identifying the right component boundaries is the single most important architectural activity; component identification precedes technology choice.
Architecture is a team sport. Conway's law means the architecture will mirror the organization's communication structure; architects must work with that reality, not against it.
ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) are the lightest-weight tool for capturing the why behind a decision. They survive staff turnover and prevent repeated re-litigation.
Modern architects must be polyglots: code-fluent, communication-fluent, and politically fluent. The days of the 'ivory tower' architect are over.
whoShouldRead: Senior engineers transitioning from implementation to architecture roles
Developers who want to understand the 'why' behind the systems they work on
Tech leads and staff/principal engineers responsible for system design
Engineering managers who need a shared vocabulary with their architects
Anyone preparing for architecture interviews at large technology companies
Practitioners looking for a vendor-neutral, pattern-rich modern reference
whoShouldSkip: Veteran architects who already have a settled taxonomy and pattern vocabulary
Programmers looking for hands-on coding guidance (this is about thinking, not typing)
Readers seeking a single 'right' answer — the book insists there isn't one
Pure managers with no technical background (the technical chapters assume fluency)

relatedBooks.title: Software Architecture: The Hard Parts
relatedBooks.author: Neal Ford, Mark Richards, Pramod Sadalage, Zhamak Dehghani
